title: Today's Tech Brief: These LLMs are the best at resisting Russian propaganda
summary: Large language models tested for resistance to Russian propaganda, US House draft bill to regulate AI, and more.

body.text: I'm looking at a fascinating study on large language models (LLMs) and their ability to resist Russian propaganda. The research tested several LLMs to see how well they could detect and reject propaganda. Some models performed significantly better than others, which is great news for those relying on AI for information.

The US House of Representatives has drafted a bill to regulate AI, according to Axios. This is a significant development, as it shows lawmakers are taking AI seriously and want to ensure it's used responsibly. The details of the bill are still emerging, but it's clear that the government is paying attention to the rapid growth of AI and its potential impact on society.

The article from Ars Technica highlights that some LLMs are better at spotting propaganda than others. I find it interesting that these models can be fine-tuned to recognize and reject certain types of misinformation. It's a bit like having a spam filter for your brain – who wouldn't want that?

My take on these developments is that AI is becoming increasingly important in our daily lives, and it's crucial for us to understand how it's being used and regulated. The fact that some LLMs can resist Russian propaganda is a promising sign that AI can be used for good. I'm excited to see how these technologies continue to evolve and shape our world.
